_comment: Edit this file to add / change / remove FAQ entries. The faq.html page reads it on every page load. Categories are free-text — group answers however helps applicants.

items.q: Which parishes are eligible for J-USE 2026?
items.a: Seven parishes: Kingston & St. Andrew, St. Catherine, Clarendon, St. Mary, St. Thomas, Westmoreland, and Hanover. Projects in other parishes can register for the future pipeline via the eligibility check.

items.q: Can individuals or unregistered groups apply?
items.a: No. J-USE requires a registered organisation (or an informal CBO with documented community recognition) AND an organisational bank account. Individuals with strong NbCS ideas should partner with a registered NGO or cooperative to host the project.

items.q: What counts as a Nature-based Climate Solution (NbCS)?
items.a:
J-USE funds projects that solve a clearly defined climate problem through nature — not against it. The intervention must use, restore, or sustainably manage a natural system or ecosystem to deliver measurable climate mitigation or adaptation and resilience outcomes, while also producing benefits for biodiversity, the wider environment, and human well-being.

Strong NbCS projects are multidimensional by design and demonstrate a systems approach — each project should show how its outcomes connect across:

  • Climate — the specific hazard(s) it addresses (drought, flood, heat, sea-level rise, etc.)
  • Nature — the ecosystem or natural system it works with and the biodiversity it influences (watershed, coastline, urban canopy, soil, mangrove, native habitat, etc.)
  • People & Livelihoods — the beneficiaries, how vulnerability is distributed, the sectors they operate in, and the communities they are a part of (women, youth, low-income, climate-exposed groups)
  • Value — the economic sector the project sits in (tourism, agriculture, fisheries, urban services, etc.) and the economic logic used (revenue-generating, public-good, or hybrid)
  • Governance and Sustainability — the institutional setup that keeps it running after the grant ends (maintenance systems, partnerships, long-term sustainability)

The form's Benefit Traceability Matrix in Section 4 is where you draw those lines explicitly — outcome by outcome.

For J-USE 2026, eligible interventions include:

  • Urban greening & heat reduction — green roofs, urban tree canopy, native pollinator landscaping
  • Water & flood management — rainwater harvesting, bioswales, constructed wetlands, greywater treatment
  • Coastal & mangrove restoration — dune stabilisation, mangrove replanting, coastal-erosion control
  • Agroforestry — urban, slope, community garden, school garden
  • Climate-smart agriculture & soil restoration

The October 2025 IUCN Global Standard for NbS — and NbCS as a subset — is the reference framework.

J-USE supports three sustainability models — all valid: revenue-generating (eco-tourism, market gardens, green-infrastructure retrofits), hybrid (revenue + public goods, e.g. agroforestry with farmer training), and public-goods (mangrove restoration, community parks). For-profit operators are explicitly welcome alongside CSOs.

items.q: Is there a minimum or maximum project budget?
items.a: There's no minimum — request whatever your project needs. The grant cap is JMD $15M per project. Larger projects can still apply if they identify confirmed co-financing partners.

items.q: Can the project run longer than 12 months?
items.a: The J-USE-funded portion of the project (primary activities) must be deliverable within 12 months. Long-term maintenance plans are required separately and demonstrate sustainability beyond the grant period.

items.q: How long does the application take?
items.a: About 45 minutes if you have your supporting documents ready. The form auto-saves every 30 seconds, and you can download a draft as JSON to resume later — even on a different computer.

items.q: What documents do I need to upload?
items.a: Most applicants upload 4 documents: (1) Letter of Intent and Authorisation, (2) Detailed Budget Breakdown using the J-USE template, (3) Organisation Registration Certificate, (4) Organisational Capacity / Previous Work (max 3 pages). Government / SOE / informal CBO applicants skip the Registration Certificate (3 documents).

items.q: What's the sample application form for?
items.a: It's a fictional but complete application for the 'Hybrid Community Agroforestry & Market Garden — Clarendon' project. Read the sample application form or click 📋 See Sample Application Form at the top of the form to load it into the form fields. You can see what good looks like, then clear and replace with your own content.

items.q: What if I lose my draft?
items.a: The form auto-saves to your browser every 30 seconds. You can also click ⬇ Download Draft (.json) to save a backup file you can re-load on any device. Apps like browser cleaners can wipe localStorage, so the JSON download is your safety net.

items.q: Can I edit my application after submitting?
items.a: Once submitted you'll receive a JUSE-2026-XXXXXX reference number. To request edits, email grants@efj.org.jm with your reference number BEFORE the May 8 deadline. After the deadline, the application is locked for review.

items.q: What happens when I click Submit?
items.a: A 3-step modal opens: (1) Save your application as PDF — opens your browser's print dialog, you choose 'Save as PDF', the file lands in your Downloads. (2) Re-attach that PDF to the submission. (3) Upload completes — you see your JUSE-2026-XXXXXX reference number on screen and receive a confirmation email with the same PDF attached.

items.q: Why do I have to save the PDF myself?
items.a: So you have a permanent local copy of exactly what EFJ receives — and so the same camera-ready PDF flows into your email confirmation, the grants team's notification, and EFJ's records. One file, one source of truth.

items.q: What happens if my submission fails?
items.a: The form has a Plan B fallback modal that opens automatically. It lets you (a) download your application as PDF, (b) email it directly to grants@efj.org.jm, or (c) retry the online submission. Your work is preserved in your browser's local storage either way.

items.q: When will I hear back?
items.a: Initial screening within 5 business days of submission. All EOI applicants are invited to four virtual webinars on May 20–21 and June 3–4, 2026. Full proposal invitations go out in late May, with the Proposal Writing Workshop following (May–June, by invitation). Grant awards announced in June 2026.

items.q: Is co-financing required?
items.a: In-kind co-financing is mandatory (volunteer time, equipment, land use, in-kind staff time). Cash co-financing is optional but strengthens an application — it demonstrates other partners share your confidence in the project.

items.q: What can in-kind co-financing include?
items.a: Anything you can put a credible monetary value on: volunteer hours at a stated hourly rate, donated land, equipment lent at no cost, contributed staff time, partner organisation contributions of materials or services, etc. Document each item in the budget template.

items.q: Are administrative costs allowed?
items.a: Yes — up to a maximum of 10% of your total project cost. The J-USE Budget Template auto-validates this cap and flags submissions that exceed it.

items.q: Can J-USE money be used for capital infrastructure?
items.a: Yes — equipment, irrigation systems, monitoring devices, post-harvest processing facilities, etc. are all eligible if they directly support the NbCS intervention. Land purchase is generally not eligible; long-term lease or community-owned arrangements are preferred.

items.q: What are the webinars?
items.a: Four virtual webinars held May 20–21 and June 3–4, 2026, covering: (1) Nature-based Climate Solutions fundamentals + IUCN criteria, (2) Value chain modelling + service models, (3) Theory of change + value chain analysis + gender/vulnerability disaggregation, (4) Sustainability mechanisms + adaptive management. Open to all EOI applicants — recordings are made available afterwards.

items.q: What is the 'future pipeline'?
items.a: EFJ is moving from one-off grant-making to portfolio management. Strong applicants who don't fit the current call (e.g. wrong parish, project too early-stage) are added to a pipeline EFJ revisits for future calls, partnerships, and matchmaking with other funders. The eligibility-check tool feeds this pipeline.

items.q: Will EFJ help me write my proposal?
items.a: Not for the EOI stage — that's intentionally lightweight. All EOI applicants are invited to the four May–June webinars (May 20–21 and June 3–4). Invited full-proposal applicants then attend a dedicated Proposal Writing Workshop (May–June, by invitation — communicated by email) for hands-on support before submitting their full proposal.

items.q: The form won't submit — what do I do?
items.a: The Plan B modal will open automatically and offer to (a) download your application as PDF, (b) email it directly to grants@efj.org.jm. Your work is preserved either way. If the modal doesn't appear, take a screenshot of the error and email grants@efj.org.jm with subject 'Submission failure' and we'll respond within 24 hours.

items.q: I lost my browser tab mid-application — is my work lost?
items.a: No. The form auto-saves to your browser's local storage every 30 seconds. Re-open the form (same browser, same device) and a prompt will offer to restore your draft. For extra safety, click ⬇ Download Draft (.json) periodically — that file works on any device.

items.q: Can I use a phone or tablet?
items.a: The form works on phones and tablets but the experience is much better on a laptop or desktop — many fields require typing longer paragraphs. We recommend laptop / desktop for the actual writing, then submit from any device.

items.q: What browser should I use?
items.a: Chrome, Safari, Edge, or Firefox — all current versions work. Internet Explorer is not supported. If the print preview doesn't open at the submission step, your browser may be blocking pop-ups — temporarily allow pop-ups for this site and try again.

items.q: What happens next?
items.a: Your submission lands in EFJ's grants portal automatically. The grants team reviews within 5 business days. All EOI applicants are invited to the four virtual webinars on May 20–21 and June 3–4, 2026. Successful EOI applicants are then invited to develop a full proposal in late May, including a dedicated Proposal Writing Workshop (May–June, by invitation). Unsuccessful applicants receive feedback and can opt into the future pipeline.

items.q: Will I get a copy of my submission?
items.a: Yes — three places: (1) the camera-ready PDF you saved at submission, (2) the email confirmation with that same PDF attached, (3) on request from grants@efj.org.jm using your reference number.

items.q: How is my data protected?
items.a: Submissions are stored in EFJ's secure Google Workspace environment (Drive + Sheets) with access limited to the J-USE grants team. Data is used only for the assessment process and EFJ's internal portfolio management. Not shared with third parties without your consent.
